author: XeanorONI
content: When you disband a fleet, it doesn’t actually transfer the cargo inside the pod and then delete the pod. It transfers ownership of the pod to the starbase. This indeed creates an accumulation of pods and you’re left wondering where the rent has gone, we have a solution ready to clean up additional pods to get the rent back. Just need to give the code a final test on Monday and if it works fine we could deploy it.

content: Indeed, this incident occurred in a very public setting, tarnishing his reputation. Allow me to elucidate the situation: this individual invests an extraordinary amount of effort and contributes significantly to the community out of genuine dedication and affection for this project. Granted, market dynamics inevitably lead to losses for us all. However, it remains an undeniable fact that he never harbored ill intentions, a fact evident to you and everyone else involved. Regrettably, he was compelled to divest his assets and absorb a financial setback due to this. author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Running nodes benefits heavily from economy of scale. It's the same thought process as cloud computing, where no matter how many users you have there is a base level of maintenance that has to be done. For computing that base could be 4 shifts to maintain it 24/7 and each shift could probably monitor and fix 100s or 1000s of machines. If I only need 1 machine I still need 4 shifts otherwise I wouldn't be on 24/7.   With nodes there's a ton of infra to build to get data from the validators to the users. The magic of software means that once you build it once it can be used for N users (there's a lot that goes in here that I'm ignoring for now). Hello Moon already built the pipeline, maintains it, and innovates on it. They can also charge a cheaper price since their R&D costs are amortized across many customers. If we build it ourselves we would have to pay all those R&D costs (including time) for ourselves only or offer it as a SAAS product.   There may be advantages to doing it ourselves but it's effectively starting a new business. If these products didn't exist and we needed them (such as last year) it would make sense to build them but since they do we can focus on what we're good at, making fun games.

If I only need 1 machine I still need 4 shifts otherwise I wouldn't be on 24/7. With nodes there's a ton of infra to build to get data from the validators to the users. The magic of software means that once you build it once it can be used for N users (there's a lot that goes in here that I'm ignoring for now). Hello Moon already built the pipeline, maintains it, and innovates on it. They can also charge a cheaper price since their R&D costs are amortized across many customers. If we build it ourselves we would have to pay all those R&D costs (including time) for ourselves only or offer it as a SAAS product. There may be advantages to doing it ourselves but it's effectively starting a new business. If these products didn't exist and we needed them (such as last year) it would make sense to build them but since they do we can focus on what we're good at, making fun games.

author: Buzzec
content: I concur with the advantages and efficiencies you've outlined regarding economies of scale and leveraging existing infrastructure solutions, especially regarding node operation and management. Hello Moon and similar entities have provided invaluable services that allow SA to remain focused on our core competency: creating the most incredible game on the planet. However, considering the upcoming exponential user growth (projections), I believe it's pivotal to anticipate a foreseeable "tipping point" within the organization. There might come a time when the costs of outsourcing these services approach or even surpass the costs of maintaining a dedicated node tailored to the game's demands/business needs.  Owning our node or validator might initially seem like a daunting venture, but it's essential to consider the broader spectrum of possibilities this could unlock. With our infrastructure, not only do we gain direct control over these types of operations, but we also open up avenues for additional revenue streams. Whether through transaction fees, validator rewards, or bespoke services, these potential earnings could offset the costs of establishing a new sub-division/department or acquiring a majority stake in an existing node service. This act could ensure that SA's efforts have dedicated coverage and specialized research that aligns seamlessly with the upcoming game's functionalities.  Today, there are 2K players. However, envision a future—given the game's trajectory—where there are tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands, or even millions of players. The numbers aren't mere pipe dreams but realistic milestones SA has mentioned that it's willing to achieve. At such scales, the economics shift significantly, and the benefits of SA having its infrastructure could far outweigh the convenience of outsourcing.  This idea isn't about deviating from SA's core mission. On the contrary, it's about proactively preparing for the success SA leadership foresees for SAGE/UE5 games. It's about ensuring that as SA grows, every aspect of the business, including backend infrastructure, scales and evolves in tandem to provide the best experience for its users without depending entirely on other companies' services (at critical junctures) for its success.  Only gains can be made in considering SA DAO proposals that engage the game's stakeholders and seek to benefit SA - as it follows its shared vision.   SA is proving to be an agile organization. While leveraging existing solutions is undeniably beneficial now, SA must also be prepared to adapt and innovate as it expands, ensuring its vital communication points are as robust and forward-thinking as the games the SA Team is expertly developing for the people (gamers).    Thank you for your time, Buzzec.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Guys. You are getting mixed up.  Running a Solana node is  completely different then running starcom or what hello moon is doing. Like night and day differences.  Running a standard Validator does nothing for SA except being a place for people to stake Solana for SA to get a cut.

SA running an RPC node is also not helpful. RPC nodes aren't fast enough.

You need complex hybrids w/ custom software that are amalgamations of RPCs databases, Validators, etc. Which is what hello moon and other services provide.

author: [HEIM] Dinarei | Vaelis.bod
content: Interesting question @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics . One thing I've noticed is how both MUD/ONI have an advantageous layout of sectors when it comes to the more basic resources you need in much larger volumes. MUD has the trio of MRZ-1, 4 and 5 with easy access to H, C, Cu and Fe at 1.5 richness all next to each other. ONI has the same with MRZ-19, 20 and 24. However, USTUR has nothing like that. In particular, there is no convenient way to get C and Fe next to each other, which complexifies some processes a lot.

The more trading the more balanced and the more win win for everyone

author: JayWalk
content: Some Ideas Targeting New & Small Fleet Owners  Tutorial & Onboarding with progressive difficulty for new players or targeting small fleet owners.,Login Bonuses for consecutive logins or time spend online.,Progression Systems as described for Starbased (Leveling Up Rank) & Career Developing (Accounts/Profiles coming),Some Instant Feedback for tasks completed and/or progress bars.,Challenges (for sense of growth and mastery - a combination of what's coming with Mobile App and Profiles).,Short & Long Term Goals - can't go wrong with having objectives and a future to promote engagement.,A reward system that's variable (SDUs and the upcoming Faction Rewards cover some of this, but we could add some Luck to it too).,Difficulty of missions tailored to fleet size --> variable rewards,SA is developing amazing Lore - let's make it more engaging for new players with a start up story.  Similar to what NA is trying to do but in SAGE.,

reply.preview: Some Ideas Targeting New & Small Fleet Owners Tutorial & Onboarding with progressive difficulty for new players or targeting small fleet owners. , Login Bonuses for consecutive logins or time spend online. , Progression Systems as described for Starbased (Leveling Up Rank) & Career Developing (Accounts/Profiles coming) , Some Instant Feedback for tasks completed and/or progress bars. , Challenges (for sense of growth and mastery - a combination of what's coming with Mobile App and Profiles). , Short & Long Term Goals - can't go wrong with having objectives and a future to promote engagement. , A reward system that's variable (SDUs and the upcoming Faction Rewards cover some of this, but we could add some Luck to it too). , Difficulty of missions tailored to fleet size --> variable rewards , SA is developing amazing Lore - let's make it more engaging for new players with a start up story. Similar to what NA is trying to do but in SAGE. ,

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: All this requires substantial development that will take away from delivering Starbased, Racing and SAGE.  Resources are fixed so allocating people to these tasks delays others things.  Personally I think when we get racing and small players just need to buy a single XXS ship to get started is when they will feel empowererd.

reply.preview: All this requires substantial development that will take away from delivering Starbased, Racing and SAGE. Resources are fixed so allocating people to these tasks delays others things. Personally I think when we get racing and small players just need to buy a single XXS ship to get started is when they will feel empowererd.

author: [AEP] Freeman [FRNT]
content:
I launched a “startmining” without food.The transaction is carried out normally on the blockchain.Obviously nothing was mined and I burned fuel for nothing... 
has anyone ever had this problem?  why was I able to make this transaction on Solana?  I should have gotten an error message because no food, right?

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: I launched a “startmining” without food.   The transaction is carried out normally on the blockchain.   Obviously nothing was mined and I burned fuel for nothing...  has anyone ever had this problem?  why was I able to make this transaction on Solana?  I should have gotten an error message because no food, right?

reply.preview: I launched a “startmining” without food. The transaction is carried out normally on the blockchain. Obviously nothing was mined and I burned fuel for nothing... has anyone ever had this problem? why was I able to make this transaction on Solana? I should have gotten an error message because no food, right?

author: [AEP] Freeman [FRNT]
content: how did you start the transaction?  I believe the UI would block this but the blockchain itself doesnt check for the required food.  Did you execute it through the UI or something else?  If it executed it through the UI it was likely the UI was out of sync with the blockchain so it didnt stop you.  There are lots of little errors like this.  Refresh and refresh often.  (Or use refresh cargo.)

reply.preview: how did you start the transaction? I believe the UI would block this but the blockchain itself doesnt check for the required food. Did you execute it through the UI or something else? If it executed it through the UI it was likely the UI was out of sync with the blockchain so it didnt stop you. There are lots of little errors like this. Refresh and refresh often. (Or use refresh cargo.)

author: [AEP] Freeman [FRNT]
content:
honestly, I don't remember clicking on the "startmining" button.
I saw it in solscan, so that's what I must have done I think.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: with UI.   I did a stop mining, then 40 seconds later a start mining. Is this problem known to the team?  Or am I the first to have it?

author: [AEP] Suto [STAR]
content: The onchain program doesn't check that, so if your UI didn't have the actual state like the starcomm didn't react, it could have happened. Much worse was when I started mining without fuel. Not through UI, but directly onchain, but in that case you have to respawn from that situation, which was not possible from the UI at that time (didn't check now)

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
Reloaded after the patch, here's some early results of testing around, just for anecdotal observations' sake.

Pressed scan, found nothing, 2.4% chance.
Moved a tile, scanned, found SDU's, 2.2% chance.
Waited a minute or so, scanned, found nothing, 6% chance.
Waited another minute, scanned, found noting, 5.8% chance.
Waited 2 minutes, scanned, 5.2% chance.

So it seems that even when finding nothing, scanning in the same place reduces the chances (though it's odd the chances went from 2.2% up to 6% when I first got to the new tile).

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: It's a race against time for the SDU as the percentage climbs.  So here's a bit of strategy.  Start with 3-5 Fleets.  Scan all.  Whatever has the highest hit chance immediately scan again as soon as possible and repeat until you hit or somebody else hits then move to the next highest.  If rates remain sub 10% and you are not seeing 20%+ then move because it is congested.

reply.preview: It's a race against time for the SDU as the percentage climbs. So here's a bit of strategy. Start with 3-5 Fleets. Scan all. Whatever has the highest hit chance immediately scan again as soon as possible and repeat until you hit or somebody else hits then move to the next highest. If rates remain sub 10% and you are not seeing 20%+ then move because it is congested.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Also, we specifically moved away from best-execution style order books when we developed this marketplace. It might not be relevant now, but as your assets evolve into NFTs, it won't make sense to have a sweep function. Or not really, unless you're speculating. Every ship is going to have custom components, mods, and crew. Or even being able to disassemble a ship and sell the hull. But even then, they'll have a unique provenance to them. At least that's the plan.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: The caveat is for these resource SFTs

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: So we might need a hybrid approach in the future. But when we developed this, we were trying to future proof it. Which is why you have to select each order individually. But it's also why you can choose any order in the order book without having to buy bottom up

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Yeah for sure man, but here’s the thing. SDU scanning is a really tough nut to crack and for it to be enjoyable for smaller players. Few reasons, it’s costly, if you’re looking to play and make a profit it’s tough and requires lots of ships. Also very repetitive and will get old and they will want to move into different loops eventually. So for a team to try and make it exciting is tough. They’ve done great with improvements though. Been testing it on the side love the interfaces they’ve added. But let’s be honest it’s a big market and bottling will be definitely mandatory to help provide to the market and they know this.

author: [AEP] Mandalorian (Djin Djarin)
content: One thing I don't see enough SA members giving praise on the purpose of the economic design which is:  The people with bigger assets have paid with money and enjoy less intensive time requirements... VS,People with less assets and more time and are willing to grind., People with money are buying time.  People with less money are paying with time.  I presume, CREW will have the same idea to a certain degree.

I have no idea what level of automation is acceptable. I don’t want to take away from others experience, but I also don’t want to windfall some other sdu Automator either

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
@[AEP] Bodhitree agree with everything you've said. The extractors  bragging about doing 100k Sdu per day pre patch and 50k+ SDUs now per is ridiculous.  And I can only assume if that's what they are telling us it's probably 2x that.  It's shameless.   And when these returns can be obtained with airbikes the capital investment is minimal compare to the gains, it's clearly not stopping anyone.

No idea how you can identify what's extractive and what's not re: Sdu.   Clearly we need some sort of additional limitations on Sdu scanning that targets extractors, such as a level based system, total fleet extraction based on vwap caps, wallet withdrawal limitations, etc etc 

Mining automation doesn't bother me since it's so easy to run manually, and it takes significant capital outlay.
